options: A set X with a collection of 'open sets' (the topology) closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections, including X and the empty set; this minimal structure defines continuity, convergence, and connectedness without any notion of distance
A set with a fixed distance function only (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Any finite set with no extra structure (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)
A space where every subset is open by definition (always) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: A topological space generalises the notion of 'nearness' beyond metric spaces by specifying directly which sets are 'open.' Formally, a topology on a set X is a collection τ of subsets (the open sets) satisfying three axioms: X and ∅ are open; arbitrary unions of open sets are open; finite intersections of open sets are open. This deceptively spare structure is enough to define the central concepts of analysis and geometry abstractly: a function is continuous iff preimages of open sets are open (no ε-δ or distance needed); convergence, closure, interior, boundary, and limit points are all defined via open sets; and global properties like connectedness (the space is not a disjoint union of two open sets) and compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover) make sense. Different topologies on the same set encode different notions of nearness (the discrete topology makes every set open; the trivial topology has only X and ∅; metric spaces induce a topology via open balls). Topology studies properties preserved under continuous deformation (homeomorphism) — 'rubber-sheet geometry' — distinguishing, e.g., a sphere from a torus. Point-set (general) topology provides the rigorous language and foundational theorems on which analysis, differential geometry, and algebraic topology are built.

active.prompt: Match each topology concept with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Preimages of open sets are open; the topological definition, no metric needed
Every open cover has a finite subcover; generalises 'closed and bounded'
Cannot be split into two disjoint nonempty open sets; one piece
Continuous bijection with continuous inverse; topological equivalence ('same shape')

active.explanation: Point-set topology abstracts analysis: continuity, compactness, and connectedness are defined purely via open sets. Homeomorphism is the notion of 'sameness' — a coffee cup and a doughnut are homeomorphic (one hole). Key theorems (Heine-Borel, Tychonoff, Urysohn) and separation axioms (Hausdorff, etc.) organise the subject.

question: Why is the topological definition of continuity powerful?

options: 'A function is continuous iff the preimage of every open set is open' needs no metric or distance, so it applies to any topological space; it makes many proofs cleaner (continuity preserved under composition trivially) and unifies analysis with geometry and beyond
It requires a metric and so is less general (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)
It only works for functions on the real line (this reverses the actual relationship) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception)
It is equivalent to the function being linear (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: The topological definition — f: X → Y is continuous iff the preimage f^{−1}(U) of every open set U ⊆ Y is open in X — is a profound generalisation of the familiar ε-δ definition, and it is powerful precisely because it is purely structural, mentioning only open sets and no distances. Its advantages: (1) Generality — it applies to any topological spaces, not just metric ones, extending continuity to function spaces, quotient spaces, manifolds, algebraic-geometry settings (Zariski topology), and infinite-dimensional spaces where no natural metric exists. (2) Elegance — many results become almost trivial: the composition of continuous maps is continuous (preimages compose); continuity is characterised by behaviour on a basis or subbasis; and key theorems (continuous image of a compact set is compact; continuous image of a connected set is connected — instantly yielding the intermediate value theorem) follow cleanly. (3) Conceptual unity — it reveals continuity as the natural notion of 'structure-preserving map' for topological spaces, paralleling homomorphisms for groups or linear maps for vector spaces, and underlies category-theoretic viewpoints. (4) It clarifies subtleties — e.g. the distinction between continuity and uniform continuity (the latter needs more than topology), and which properties are topological invariants. This abstraction, due to the development of general topology in the early 20th century, is foundational across modern mathematics, letting analysts, geometers, and algebraists speak a common language of continuity.

question: What is the difference between connectedness and compactness?

options: Connectedness means a space is 'one piece' (not a union of two disjoint nonempty open sets); compactness means every open cover has a finite subcover ('no escaping to infinity / no missing limit points'). Both are topological invariants preserved by continuous maps but capture different ideas
They are the same property (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Compact sets are always disconnected (this conflates cause and effect) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship)
Connected sets are always finite (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Connectedness and compactness are two of the most important topological properties, capturing genuinely different intuitions, though both are preserved under continuous maps. Connectedness formalises 'being in one piece': a space is connected if it cannot be partitioned into two disjoint, nonempty open sets (equivalently, the only sets that are both open and closed are the whole space and the empty set). The continuous image of a connected set is connected — which immediately yields the intermediate value theorem (a continuous real function on an interval, being connected, cannot skip values). Path-connectedness (any two points joined by a continuous path) is a related, slightly stronger notion. Compactness formalises a kind of 'finiteness' or 'no leaking away': every open cover admits a finite subcover (in metric spaces, equivalent to being closed and bounded, or to every sequence having a convergent subsequence). The continuous image of a compact set is compact, giving the extreme value theorem (a continuous function on a compact set attains its max and min). Compactness lets infinite arguments reduce to finite ones and is the workhorse of existence proofs. A space can be connected but not compact (the real line), compact but not connected (two disjoint closed intervals), both (a closed interval [0,1]), or neither (two disjoint open rays). Together with separation axioms (Hausdorff etc.), these invariants classify and distinguish spaces, and they are central tools throughout analysis, geometry, and beyond.

question: What does the classification of surfaces say?

options: Every closed (compact, boundaryless) surface is homeomorphic to exactly one of: a sphere, a connected sum of g tori (orientable, genus g), or a connected sum of k projective planes (non-orientable); they are fully classified by orientability and Euler characteristic / genus
All surfaces are homeomorphic to a sphere (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Surfaces cannot be classified (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Only orientable surfaces exist (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: The classification of closed surfaces is one of the most satisfying complete results in topology: every compact, connected surface without boundary falls into exactly one homeomorphism class, determined by just two invariants — orientability and a single integer. The orientable surfaces are the sphere (genus 0), the torus (genus 1, one 'hole'), the double torus (genus 2), and in general the connected sum of g tori (genus g, g holes). The non-orientable surfaces are the real projective plane, the Klein bottle, and their generalisations (connected sums of k projective planes), which cannot be embedded in 3-space without self-intersection and have no consistent notion of 'clockwise.' Equivalently, surfaces are classified by their Euler characteristic χ (χ = 2 − 2g for an orientable genus-g surface; χ = 2 − k for the non-orientable ones) together with orientability — and χ is computable from any triangulation as V − E + F. This means two closed surfaces are homeomorphic iff they have the same orientability and the same Euler characteristic — a complete, computable classification. The result (essentially complete by the early 20th century) is a model for the much harder classification problems in higher dimensions: 3-manifolds were classified only via Thurston's geometrisation and Perelman's proof, while 4-manifolds remain wild and manifolds of dimension ≥ 5 are governed by surgery theory. The surface classification beautifully illustrates how global topological type is captured by a few discrete invariants.

question: What is a homeomorphism and what does 'topological invariant' mean?

options: A homeomorphism is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse — topology's notion of 'sameness' (rubber-sheet deformation); a topological invariant (connectedness, compactness, genus, Euler characteristic, fundamental group) is a property preserved by homeomorphisms, used to distinguish spaces
A homeomorphism is any function between spaces (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence)
Topological invariants change under continuous deformation (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
A homeomorphism must preserve distances exactly (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: A homeomorphism is the central notion of equivalence in topology: a bijection f: X → Y that is continuous and whose inverse is also continuous. Two spaces related by a homeomorphism are 'topologically the same' — one can be continuously deformed into the other without cutting or gluing (hence topology's nickname 'rubber-sheet geometry'). The classic example: a coffee mug and a doughnut are homeomorphic, since each has exactly one hole and one can be moulded into the other. Importantly, homeomorphism does not preserve metric notions like distance, angle, or curvature (those belong to geometry); it preserves only purely topological structure. A topological invariant is any property or quantity that is preserved under homeomorphism — so if two spaces differ in some invariant, they cannot be homeomorphic. Key invariants include: connectedness and path-connectedness, compactness, the separation axioms (Hausdorff, etc.), dimension, the Euler characteristic, genus (number of holes), orientability, and the algebraic invariants of algebraic topology — the fundamental group π_1 (loops up to deformation) and the homology/homotopy groups. Invariants are the practical tools for telling spaces apart: e.g. the circle and the line differ because removing a point disconnects the line but not the circle; the sphere and torus differ in genus/Euler characteristic and in π_1 (trivial for the sphere, ℤ² for the torus). The interplay between finding new invariants and using them to classify spaces drives much of topology, with algebraic topology providing the most powerful computable invariants.

question: What is the Brouwer fixed-point theorem and why is it important?

options: Any continuous map from a closed ball (or any compact convex set) to itself has at least one fixed point f(x)=x; a deep topological existence result with applications in analysis, differential equations, game theory (Nash equilibria), and economics (general equilibrium)
Continuous self-maps never have fixed points (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect)
It applies only to linear maps (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
It requires the map to be a contraction (not supported by the evidence) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: The Brouwer fixed-point theorem (1911) states that every continuous function f from a compact, convex set (paradigmatically the closed n-dimensional ball, or equivalently a disk) to itself has at least one fixed point — a point x with f(x) = x. In one dimension it follows from the intermediate value theorem (a continuous map of [0,1] to itself must cross the diagonal); in higher dimensions it is genuinely topological, with elegant proofs via homology, the non-existence of a retraction of the ball onto its boundary sphere, or combinatorial arguments (Sperner's lemma). Intuitive illustrations: if you crumple a flat map and lay it on top of its original (within the same region), some point lies exactly over its original position; stirring a cup of coffee, some fluid particle returns to its starting spot (in the appropriate idealisation). Its importance is far-reaching: it is the prototypical non-constructive existence theorem in nonlinear analysis (guaranteeing solutions to equations f(x)=x without finding them), underpins existence proofs for differential and integral equations (alongside its generalisations — the Schauder fixed-point theorem in infinite-dimensional Banach spaces, and the Kakutani fixed-point theorem for set-valued maps). Most famously, Kakutani's generalisation is the key to Nash's proof that every finite game has a Nash equilibrium (Nobel-winning game theory), and Brouwer/Kakutani fixed points are the mathematical foundation of the Arrow-Debreu general-equilibrium theorem in economics. The theorem beautifully shows how a purely topological fact about continuous maps yields powerful, concrete consequences across mathematics, economics, and the social sciences.
